edibility: Eaten in some Southeast Asian, Mexican, and East African cuisines despite tough texture; Western mycology considers it inedible/medicinal; rare opportunistic infections in immunocompromised individuals are the primary concern.
taste: Mild, slightly woody flavor with a characteristically tough, rubbery texture that persists even after cooking.
search: Year-round on rotting hardwood worldwide, particularly on fallen logs and dead trees. Identify by distinctive split radial gills (bifurcate appearance), fan or funnel-like shape, and whitish to gray coloring. Similar genera include Trichaptum and Stereum, distinguished by gill structure and spore prints.

toxicity.symptoms: Rare invasive infection in immunocompromised individuals; otherwise no toxicity
toxicity.onset: Days to weeks if invasive infection develops
toxicity.treatmentWindow: Immediate medical intervention required for invasive infections
